videos.title: DON'T Make these Mistakes in Istanbul
videos.description:
Istanbul is unforgettable, unless you accidentally ruin it.
In this video, Saygın breaks down 12 of the most common mistakes tourists make when visiting Istanbul. These are small decisions that can lead to big frustrations, wasted money, or missed experiences.

You’ll learn why relying on taxis, skipping the Istanbulkart, or staying only in tourist-heavy areas can affect your trip more than you expect. The video also covers what to wear at mosques, how to find real local food, and why exploring beyond the European side matters. These insights come from years of living in the city and seeing what visitors often get wrong.

Whether it’s your first visit or you’ve been here before, this guide will help you avoid stress and make the most of your time in Istanbul.

✔ 12 tips covering transportation, money, food, neighborhoods, and cultural awareness
✔ How to avoid tourist traps and move around more easily

You can buy it from any metro station Kios and recharge it easily. Seriously, don't be that trist digging for coins every time they take a ride. It's not just about saving money. It's about moving like a local. When you use the stumble card, you unlock the entire city. It makes your day smoother and way more fun. Mistake two, taking random taxes. Here's the deal with taxis in stumble. Some are great, others not so much. I've seen tourists get charged triple just because they didn't notice the driver didn't turn on the meter. And if they see you are a tourist who doesn't know the usual prices, they might take this scenic route. So use apps like B taxi, Uber, and always make sure that meter is on when you get in. Also, if driver says no meter, fixed price, that's your sign to get out. And here is the thing, stumble traffic is already stressful. The last thing you want is to argue with the driver about price. The apps protect you and with them you also avoid the awkward language barrier and have a record of the trip. Way less stress. Mistake three, not carrying cash. I know we are all used to paying by cards, but still has those little moments where the cash is king. like you want to buy rusted chestnuts or use the public toilet or the cart reader is magically broken. Don't want to be stuck asking people to break a big note or worse get ripped off because you only have foreign currency. Just keep a bit of Turkish LRA and some coins in your pocket. And one tip, don't exchange a lot of money at airport. The rates in the city are way better. Having a little cash on you keeps things moving. No skipping that amazing street snack just because they don't take cards. It gives you freedom. Mistake four, not getting a SIM or EIM. Let's be honest, you are going to need internet for Google Maps, translating menus, booking a ride, even figuring out if you are walking in the right direction, but airport sim card shops crazy expensive. Go to an actual TXL or Vodafone store in the city. Or if you want it even easier, use an eim. If you want to try, I left a link in description. It gives you a discount, too. With data, stumble becomes 10 times easier. It's like having a professional guide in your pocket. You will move smarter, eat better, and avoid mistakes without even trying. Mistake five. Now, listen. You don't need to speak fully in Turkish, okay? But a couple of words, they go a long way. Even saying mehaba or tesakular makes a difference. People light up when you try and sometimes you even get better service. It's like a little thing but it shows respect and that matters here. Even a few Turkish words can unlock smiles. Locals will appreciate the effort and suddenly the interaction becomes more than just a train section. It becomes connection. All right. Those first five were all about transportation, money and staying connected. Now for the last seven mistakes, we are going to focus on something just as important. How you experience is stumble. That means culture, food, neighborhoods, and even what you wear. This is where most people get it wrong without even realizing it. Mistake six, wearing the wrong shoes. Istanul has seven heels. Seven. I've seen people in sandals, heels by lunchtime and they are limping. Do your future self a favor and a very good walking shoes. You are gonna walk a lot, but it's worth it. Trust me, IstAnul rewards walkers. You will discover hidden spots, secret viewpoints, and quiet streets full of magic, but only if your feet survive the day. Mistake seven, trying to do too much in one day. This city is massive and beautiful. But if you are trying to see 10 places in one day, you will end up tired, hungry, annoyed. Instead of enjoying Dio Sophia, you will just be checking the time. So here my advice, pick two or three things. Explore them slowly. Sip tea, watch people. That's how you really feel stumble. You'll come back with stories, not just photos. The less you rush, the more the city gives you back. It's not a checklist, it's an experience. Mistake eight, buying all souvenirs in the Grand Bazar. Now, don't get me wrong, the Grand Bazar is amazing. It's like a living museum, but prices there tourist prices. If you buy everything there, you'll pay double what it's worth. For better deals, head to Kadikoy or Arasto Bazar near the Blue Mos. some stuff more local prices. Also, in smaller bazars, you can actually talk to the shop owners. They'll tell you stories about the crafts, maybe even offer tea. That's when you're shopping becomes part of the trip, not just the train section. Mistake nine, dressing wrong for mosque. You walk up to a mosque and then they stop you at the door. Why? Shoulders showing, knees out or no headcarves? Most mosque will give you something to cover up. But if you already have a scarf and long pants, you are good to go. Respect the space and you will have a way better experience. Mosques are more than buildings. They are leaving places of faith. When you dress appropriately, you are not just following rules. You are showing respect and in return you are welcomed in with open arms. Mistake 10. only staying on European side. Everyone knows Sutan Ahmed Takim. That's where the big names are. But Istanul has two sides. The Asian side, especially Kaduko, is full of locals, way less tourist, and honestly has better food. It's peaceful, it's cheap, and it's really stumble. You can sit by the water with tea in your hand, surrounded by locals. No rush, no noise, just the real heartbeat of the city. And the f ride over it's a whole wipe. Sea breeze seagulls. It's unforgettable. Mistake 11. Skipping street food. Look, some of the best meals you will eat in a stumble come from a cart. Simmit don cocker stuffed muscles. They are everywhere. If locals are eating from that stand tent, it is probably safe. And just avoid places that look empty or dirty. And please don't leave IstAnul without trying Midia Dolma at least once. Street food is the taste of the real city. It's fast, it's local, and it's full of soul. Plus, it's where you'll try things you never find in a restaurant menu. Mistake 12, eating at trap restaurants. If someone is shouting, "My friend, best food, come in." Just walk away. Real local restaurants don't pull people in like that. Look for places where locals are actually eating. No limited menus with 10 flags, just good food, crowded tables, maybe even no English signs. That's where the magic is. The best meals happen where the food speaks for itself. You'll taste home-cooked flavors and feel like you are part of the neighborhood. And that's it. These are the top 12 mistakes trist make in Istanbul. If you can avoid just a few of them, you will save money and have a smoother trip and honestly enjoy this city so much more. videos.transcript: If you only have a day or two to see Istanbul, chances are you’ll hit the classics — the top 10 "I'll call them" "postcard activities": Hagia Sophia. The Blue Mosque. A stroll down Istiklal Cadesi Street all the way down to Taksim Square Climb Galata Tower, maybe. Though once is enough at $35 a pop. Get lost in the Grand Bazaar Grab yourself one too many samples in the Egyptian Spice Bazaar. Visit the stunning Topkapi Palace Go underground to explore the 336 columns of Basilica Cistern. And of course, indulge in the food. [Music] You’ll knock most of these out in Sultanahmet, the core tourist area of the Fatih district. As well as in Beyoğlu, the more modern center of Istanbul on the European side. And sure, they’re iconic. They’re beautiful. But they’re also crowded, catered mostly to tourists, and in many ways, exactly what you'd expect. But...if you can stick around a little longer —and I recommend at least a week - the city really starts to crack open. You start seeing Istanbul, not just visiting it. And even if you're here for a short time there are so many lesser known activities and more authentic experiences that you can do that locals also love doing. So here's my list of the top 20 things you should do to get a more well-rounded experience of the real Istanbul that most tourists overlook. And if you're looking for a comprehensive map highlighting our favorite food, coffee, and activities you can't miss, we'll have that linked below. Take the ferry just to take a ferry Besides the food, Istanbul's ferry system is our favorite thing about this city. For about 1 US Dollar, you can take a ferry to get to many of the different Istanbul neighborhoods and districts. The views are outrageous, we would sometimes just get on the ferry for the experience itself. And many locals do the same. There are three major ferry operators in the city: the municipally owned Şehir Hatları which translates to "City Lines", which operates traditional vapurs; the privately operated İstanbul Deniz Otobüsleri which translates to "İstanbul Sea Busses" which operates high-speed urban and intercity services, and the privately owned Turyol which operates mostly urban services.Taking a ferry between continents especially in the early mornings or late evenings during sunset is magical...and the city glows at golden hour. Visit Ferry Terminal Library Cafes Another interesting thing locals did that we loved was hang out at one of the many different ferry terminal libraries and cafes. Yes, can you believe it, their ferry terminals have their own subsidized library and cafe with reasonable prices and incredible views right on the waterfront. Something we never knew about but we're stoked when we discovered it. Everyone knows how hilly Istanbul can be, especially the Beyloğlu district on the European side. But what not everyone knows is that there's an epic shortcut that skips the big hill and takes you straight to Istiklal Caddesi street. All right, we are heading now to take the Tunel which is the second oldest underground railway in the world. It's uh basically a 90 second ride on this finicular that will take you from the bottom of Karakoy all the way to Istiklal Caddesi street. So, it's kind of like a shortcut to save you from that ginormous hill. Uh, which is honestly so clutch because on a day like today, uh, I would be drenched by the time I would get to Istiklal. And it's very affordable. So, it's a great way to save yourself that hill. Even exiting a steep. Yeah. This got us to Istiklal Caddesi Street. The tower is only like 400 m away. [Music] Explore the best coffee shops and antique stores in Cihangir. Cihangir is probably the coolest sub-neighborhood in the Beyoğlu district. It's known for it's cafes, antique stores, bohemian laid-back culture, an abundance of street cats... The only downside of this area is it's hilly AF. The streets are steep. To get to the heart of it, I recommend typing in "Berol Antik" in Google Maps to get you there. Maybe pop in to Cuma to try Cilbir, a popular breakfast dish that's delicious. But honestly, there are so many good spots to eat and drink, you can't go wrong. And like I said, when it comes to great coffee shops, Cihangir has them everywhere, here's a list of our personal favorites. [Music] Visit the Kusgun neighborhood in Uscadar. Kusgunduk, nestled in the Uscadar district on the Asian side, is a picturesque cobblestone neighborhood blending history, culture, and tranquility. It has a very mellow, villagel-like, neighborhoody vibe. We stopped several times to grab some truly outstanding coffee and had what we thought was the best manta in the whole city made by a very sweet older woman. Walk along their main street, Iadia Street, and you'll find many artisan bakeries, cozy cafes, antique shops, and unique stores capturing that oldworld vibe. I also recommend taking a stroll along the Uscodar Coast Walkway, especially during Golden Hour, where you can enjoy views of the Galata Tower across the water and get an uplose look of Maiden's Tower. Walk along the shore of Beck. Bec is an elegant upscale neighborhood on the European shore of the Bosphorus in Istanbul's Bashikbash district. Known as Bazun Guz B, the apple of the Bosphorus's eye, it has long been one of the city's most prestigious residential areas dating back to Ottoman times. This area has great coffee shops and restaurants, but what I like most about it is its coastal walk right along the waterfront. This is hands down one of the best running routes in the city. You can literally run all the way from Ortico Mosque to Beck Park. And if you want to take a dip in the Bosphorus, this area in front of Beck Park is literally the best place near the city center that's safe and protected from boats. From there, you can also walk south to a very charming area called Arnavitkay, which translates to Albanian village, or even head north to explore one of the many fortresses. They're absolutely stunning and another reminder of Istanbul's long history. Do a street food tour on the Asian side. A street food safari with a guide is a must here in Estambul, but I particularly recommend doing one on the Asian side in Kazik. Having a local show you around can definitely give you valuable insider information that you wouldn't otherwise know and also give you a good foundation to base yourself off of to explore on your own. Some top foods I 100% recommend you try are media. They're classic stuffed muscles that come either regular or spicy and somehow taste even better when they feed it to you. Tantuni durum stir-fried lamb, beef, or chicken served in these long skinny snorkel-shaped wraps. Lak majun, one of our favorites. A delicious flatbread topped with minced meat, vegetables, and herbs. Ped looks like a boat-shaped pizza made with wheat-based dough and topped with cheese, tomatoes, and the meat of your choosing. Manta. This is a type of dumpling found a lot in Turkish cuisine. Typically coated with a yogurt garlic sauce. So so good. Tersu. Turks know how to mix an acid into their meals. These pickled vegetables are often offered alongside dishes. The famous and very strong Turkish coffee served with finely ground coffee beans still in it that settle to the bottom. Do not consume that part. And of course try baklava and so many of their other desserts. And if you're more independent and want to go at it solo, consider doing a self-guided route, which I will be also making a video on in the weeks to come. On a side note, for those looking for great shopping in Istanbul, Kadik, in my opinion, has the best shopping and the best prices in the city. And fun fact, Zara and H&M actually make a lot of their clothes here in Turkey. Have a raka and mez night at a Mayhane. Istanul is synonymous with mayhan. A traditional local drink house that's the go-to spot to drink alcohol with mez. This is a must do local experience. Long dinners with various small plates, sampler style Turkish music, and the national spirit, raka. It doesn't get any better than that. You can either go to a more traditional maze bar or a more modern new wave mayane. Most tourists commonly go to rooftop bars in Sultanameit. Locals go to mayhanes. And don't forget to dilute your raka to your preferred level. Have a Turkish breakfast experience aka the sultan special with locals. If you want to know what it feels like to eat like a sultan and experience all the gluttony and hedonism that comes with it. Have an abundant Turkish breakfast called Cavala with some locals and have them lead the way. I recommend going to either the charming Jihunger neighborhood that I just mentioned or go to the main breakfast streets in Bishikash. Finish it off with a nice cup of Turkish coffee or chai. Get lost in Balot and Fener's colorful streets. One morning we chose to take the ferry from Kadikay to Fenire. The journey took us about a half an hour and the views as always were incredible from the ferry as you get to go a bit deeper into the Golden Horn and go under Galata Bridge as well as under Ataturk Bridge. It was nice to be able to get that unique macro view of Finer and Balot from the water before actually getting to visit it on foot. Known as the Jewish and Greek quarters of Istanul, these neighborhoods give you a true window into Istanbul's layered past. From Byzantine to Jewish to Greek and Ottoman, here you'll find lively cobbled streets, rainbow colored Ottoman houses, probably what it's best known for, charming coffee shops, lots of vintage stores, antique stores, and small, tastefully curated boutiques. We sat down to enjoy the AC at a really good independent coffee shop called Coffee Department. This area of the city deserves at the very least its own afternoon to explore. We did notice as soon as we arrived that there were a lot of tour groups that come here. It can feel a bit Instagrammy. Stuff like this feels a bit tourist trappy to me, but the neighborhood itself and its buildings are very charming. And you can just explore it at your own pace and skip stuff like this, but it for sure has a good selection of restaurants and coffee shops to choose from. Here's where we stopped for lunch. Perfect. Thank you. Just sat down at a spot called Kufta Bud. They specialize in meatballs. It came with all these toppings. Got some spicy onions, eggplant, tomatoes, grilled green peppers. Olivia went with the chesta meatball wrap. Came with some of this flatbread. It looks amazing. And then on the side, I also ordered an ishi chesta. Oh my god, look at the meat in there. Real spicy brown beef, onions, perfectly crispy on the outside, oily, rich, delicious. Get a full service traditional Turkish barber experience. The most insane barber experience I've ever had was in Istanul. It was a roller coaster of surprises. The actual haircut and shave took all of about 15 minutes. But what followed after was the real treat. Facial steam, exfoliation, ultrasonic, microdermabbrasion, nose wax, flame ear hair removal. Yeah, that was a bit of a shocker. Face and hair masks, some kind of spray, some kind of after shave, some shampoo action, head massage, face massage, neck massage, more head rubbing, that claw head massage tool that makes your toes curl, and the guy even gave me a back massage to finish it all off. I walked out feeling like I'd been reborn. Only in IstAnul, only at a Turkish barber. For the full experience, ask for a comp bakum, which means complete care, which will include cut, shave, wash, and afterare. [Music] Player watch a traditional game in a local cave or cafe. A cavehane directly translates to coffee shop, but is essentially a place where older people go to play board games for hours and drink Turkish tea. The younger generation usually just plays games at regular cafes. But this is one thing that immediately stood out to me and I just loved observing. So many people would play games with each other. No phones, just actual real connection. The two most popular games we saw being played were Tavla, which is Bagaman, and the tilebased game called Okay, which is part of the Ry family. If you're bold enough, maybe ask a local to teach you one of the local games so you can join in. I chickenened out, but I wish I hadn't. Here are a few bonus recommendations. I'll rapid fire through lightning round style. Have a traditional tea garden experience. Chai is everything to a stumble and such a big part of everyday life. They seriously drink so many every day. That's why I recommend you visit a traditional tea garden like Moda Alle Chai Bachesi for a very local way to enjoy chai. Sitting in white plastic chairs while sipping tea and looking out at the sea of Marmura. You've got accordion players and some meat sellers nearby. It's a vibe. Got my sea meat. Book a weekday morning slot in a hamom for the most tranquil experience. When visiting Istanbul, I recommend you go to at least one homam. This one was great, but probably not the most authentic, but it was the only one that allowed us to film. Don't forget to also check out the Arsta Bazaar right behind the Blue Moss. We have made it to Arsta Bazaar. It has a lot less people than the Grand Bazaar or Spice Bazaar, and the offering is overall better in my opinion. I recommend going to Jennifer's Collection for the best selection of towels, blankets, and robes. And when you go to the Grahams, instead of sticking to the main corridors, ask shopkeepers or follow side alleys to discover some of the hidden courtyards. Type Zenzir Lehan in Google Maps to get directions to one of our favorites. Some have rooftop access and secret tea spots. Don't forget to visit the Istanul Museum of Modern Art, the Dolabach Palace, and the Ort Mosque. Walk up to Chamluka Hill for a great viewpoint of the city. If you have more time, check out one of the prince's islands. Burgazada is the biggest one and swimming friendly but can get busy. Kinalyada has narrow pebbled beaches lined with white wooden houses. And Helada is the second biggest island and the one I was recommended the most by locals. If you do want to visit the underground Basilica Sistern, consider visiting it at night doing what's called the night shift experience for a surreal ambience with fewer crowds. The place takes on a haunting magical feel after dark with lighting and music. I also recommend doing a quick vintage tram ride down Istigla Jadesi Street, but I especially recommend doing it also at night. Rolling through the glowing chaos of Istigla Street on the old nostalgic red tram is an iconic experience. And lastly, one of my personal favorite free things to do is to just hang out with the fisherman on Galata Bridge while soaking in the stunning views. We did this when we were there in winter and really enjoyed it. I hope this video was helpful and gave you some unique ideas of things to do in Istanbul. I will be linking our full map below with every single food spot and coffee shop we recommend. Istanbul is truly an incredible city that I hope everyone gets a chance to visit at some point in their lives. And if you want to support our channel, please subscribe and like this video to boost that algorithm. [Music]

videos.title: Visiting Istanbul? Do NOT Stay In Sultanahmet (Do This Instead)
videos.description:
Thinking of staying in Sultanahmet on your Istanbul trip? You might want to think again.
It’s close to all the big landmarks — Hagia Sophia, Blue Mosque, Topkapi Palace — but that convenience comes with trade-offs that many tourists don’t realize until it’s too late. In this video, Saygın shares 4 key reasons why Sultanahmet might actually hurt your experience, based on years of local insight and real conversations with visitors who stayed there and regretted it.

From overpriced restaurants and tourist-only shops to quiet, empty streets at night, Sultanahmet can feel more like a museum than a real neighborhood. You’ll also hear about two much better options — Beyoğlu and Kadıköy — both full of local life, real food, great transport, and the kind of energy that makes Istanbul unforgettable.

✔ Where locals actually live, eat, and hang out
✔ How to avoid tourist traps and still stay central
✔ Neighborhood comparisons based on vibe, cost, transport, and nightlife
✔ Tips to choose the best area for your style of travel

Whether it’s your first time or you’re looking for a deeper experience, this guide will help you avoid the usual mistakes and find your place in the real Istanbul.

videos.transcript: Traveling to Istanbul in 2026? Here's everything you need to know about the new fees, price increases, and travel costs so you can budget smarter. All right, let's start with some of Istanbul's most famous landmarks and their entrance fees for 2026. Spoiler alert: some of these might shock you. Hagia Sophia: In 2025, the entrance fee was €25. In 2026, it remains the same at €25. Now, I have seen a lot of people saying that the ticket costs €50 instead of 25, but that information isn't accurate, and I'll explain why. There's a museum called the Hagia Sophia Experience Museum located near the Hippodrome. When you line up to buy tickets to Hagia Sophia, you'll see ads with QR codes encouraging you to purchase tickets online and skip the line. However, it's important to know that the tickets sold online are combined tickets. They include access to the Byzantine Mosaic Gallery inside Hagia Sophia and the Hagia Sophia Experience Museum. Those combined tickets are the ones that cost €50 per person. That's why I always recommend that if you only want to visit the Mosaic Gallery inside Hagia Sophia, you should wait in line and purchase a ticket directly at the ticket office. They are not sold online. That way, you'll pay €25, not 50. Another important thing to note is that Hagia Sophia entered a restoration period a few months ago. It's expected to last several years, although there's no confirmed timeline yet. As a result, large portions of the monument are currently covered and can't be admired. As usual, you can still choose to pay to visit the Byzantine Mosaic Gallery, but the views from the upper level down to the main floor are blocked. So, it's worth keeping the current situation in mind before you go. Basilica Cistern: In 2026, the entrance fee has increased from 1,300 Turkish Lira to 1,950 Lira per person for day visits between 9:00 in the morning to 6:30 p.m., and 3,000 Lira per person for night visits between 7:30 p.m. and 10:00 p.m. Galata Tower: The entrance fee for Galata Tower remains unchanged in 2026 at €30. Topkapi Palace: Previously 2,400 Turkish Lira in 2025, the price has now increased to 2,750 Lira in 2026. The ticket includes entrance to the palace, the Harem, and Hagia Irene. Dolmabahçe Palace: In 2025, its entrance fee was 1,800 Lira, but as of 2026, the entrance fee increased to 2,000 Lira per person. Another important update for this palace museum is that photography and video recording are now allowed inside the premises. So you can finally snap some beautiful photos. And trust me, this place is truly majestic. Beylerbeyi Palace: In 2025, the price was 700 Lira. In 2026, it has increased to 800 Lira per person. A more reasonable rate, but still a rise from last year. Many people have also asked about the prices of the different museum passes offered by the Ministry of Culture and Tourism in Turkey. For now, most prices remain unchanged since the last update. They are as follows: Museum Pass Istanbul €105; Museum Pass Cappadocia €65; Museum Pass Mediterranean €90; Museum Pass Aegean €95; and Museum Pass Turkiye €165. If you want to learn in detail which museums and regions each pass covers, how long they are valid, where to purchase them, and more, I invite you to watch this video where I explain everything. The link is in the description box and pinned comment. And before we continue with the video, let me introduce you to Nomad, my favorite way to stay connected while traveling abroad completely hassle-free. It's super affordable, works in over 200 destinations—including Turkey—and even offers regional plans. All you have to do is download the app, choose a plan, and activate it for instant internet as soon as you land. Just make sure your phone is unlocked and supports eSIMs. And here's a bonus: use my code for $5 off your first purchase and explore with peace of mind. Oh, and if you have heard that eSIMs don't work in Turkey after the ban, here's the secret they don't tell you: just buy, install, and activate your eSIM before your trip, and you'll have instant data the moment you arrive. We have tested it, and it works perfectly. If you're visiting, the best, easiest, and most affordable way to get around the city is by public transportation. Here are the new prices officially announced on February 13 and effective as of February 16, 2026. Metros, buses, trams, Tünel, funiculars, and cable cars are now 42 Lira per ride. Marmaray, which is a train connecting Europe and Asia, is 74.70 Lira for a full ride. Ferries: single ride ticket prices have gone up as well, and the prices depend on the route one is taking. I'll mention a few of the most common routes tourists usually take with Şehir Hatları: Beşiktaş to Kadıköy is 59.28 Lira; Eminönü to Kadıköy is 59.28 Lira; Üsküdar to Eminönü is 53.20 Lira. For this, you will need a special card called Istanbulkart. The plastic card itself costs 165 Turkish Lira and has a monthly top-up limit. If you want to learn more about where to purchase it and how to easily top it up, you can watch this other video linked in the description box and pinned comment. If you're spending a short time in Istanbul and don't want to purchase and top up an Istanbulkart, you can opt for electronic passes (Limited-Use Cards) which come in different packages. These can also be purchased from the yellow machines in the metro and tram stations and ferry ports. On some metro, tram, and even ferry lines, credit cards are accepted as a form of payment. However, the fare is significantly higher compared to using an Istanbulkart. It's also important to note that not every station supports credit card payments yet. So, I do not recommend relying solely on your card for transportation. To save both money and time, make sure to have an Istanbulkart with you. If you must take a taxi, here are the fares you should be aware of. The taxi meter starts at 65.40 Lira. The rate per kilometer is 43.56 Lira. But most importantly, the minimum fare (indi-bindi) is now 210 Lira. This means that even if your trip is just 1 kilometer or less, you'll still have to pay this minimum amount. If you're looking to explore Istanbul and dive deeper into its fascinating history, joining a group or private tour is a great option. Here are the approximate prices to keep in mind for 2026: group tours without museum entrance fees start from $50 to $60 per person. Group tours including museum tickets start from $80 to $90 per person approximately. Keep in mind that some group tours can include up to 40 people and are usually not flexible with timing or itinerary. Private tours range from $100 to $140 per day depending on the number of people in your group. These offer much more flexibility in scheduling and itinerary as they are exclusively for you and your travel companions. Depending on your budget, you can choose the option that best suits your needs. If you're looking for private tours in Istanbul or other cities in Turkiye, check out our services. The link and WhatsApp contact are in the description box and pinned comments. A crucial factor to consider when planning a travel budget is estimating the approximate cost of meals. The most touristy areas of Istanbul like Sultanahmet, Taksim, İstiklal, and Galata tend to have significantly higher prices compared to local neighborhoods. Let's take a look at the prices of some popular street snacks: Simit, the sesame-covered ring-shaped bread commonly sold in red carts across the city, currently costs 20 Lira. Grilled corn, a classic Turkish street food, costs 70 Lira in tourist areas. Roasted chestnuts range from 100 Turkish Lira to 500 Lira depending on the quantity. In Sultanahmet, one of Istanbul's busiest tourist districts, a meal at a mid-range restaurant typically costs between 700 to 1,500 Lira for different types of kebabs. If you're looking for breakfast near tourist attractions, expect to pay anywhere from 1,000 to 1,500 Lira for a traditional Turkish breakfast. For those interested in fine dining, here's an example: This restaurant located in the heart of Sultanahmet specializes in Ottoman cuisine, featuring dishes that were once prepared for Topkapi Palace during the Ottoman Empire. It has also earned recognition from the Michelin Guide. For this type of establishment, here is what you can expect to pay. However, you can still find places with local prices even in Sultanahmet. One of our personal favorites is Köş Kebap. These are their current prices—pretty much the same as the restaurants near our home. If you're looking for delicious kebabs at fair prices, this is definitely one of those spots. For travelers on a tight budget, the best option is to eat in local neighborhoods where prices are significantly lower. Another great alternative is to dine at Lokantas—traditional Turkish eateries offering homestyle meals at affordable prices. One of my favorites is Balkan Lokantası, which has several branches, including locations near Sultanahmet and Taksim. And that's our 2026 Istanbul travel update. What do you guys think? Are these prices still reasonable or are things getting too expensive? Let me know in the comments and don't forget to like, subscribe, and hit the notification bell so you don't miss the next videos about Istanbul. See you in the next one. Bye-bye!
